blocks.content: Grief doesn't leave on a schedule. It doesn't wait for you to be ready, doesn't give notice, doesn't check whether you have somewhere to be. It moves through a house the way weather does — present one morning and gone the next, then back again without warning in the middle of an otherwise ordinary Tuesday.

They called Dr. Reeves — not for a session, just to talk. She listened. She said the things worth saying: that grief shared is not grief halved but grief witnessed, which is the only thing that makes it survivable. She asked if they were still moving toward each other. They said yes. She said that was enough.

Daisy's book helped. Not because writing was an escape — she'd never believed in that version of it — but because the work required her to stay present, to be precise about what she felt, and precision had a way of making things smaller. Not small. Just more possible to hold.

Kit threw himself into a pitch. A short film — something he'd been orbiting for months, a story about a boy and a field and a town that existed nowhere on a map. He didn't tell Daisy what it was really about. Not yet.

By December they had begun to laugh again. Not performed laughter — real laughter, the kind that surprised you. They cooked elaborate things on Sunday nights and made bad decisions about wine and watched old films on the couch with Daisy's legs across his lap and the city going dark outside the window.
It wasn't the life they'd imagined in September. It was a different life — one that had been tested and was still standing.
That was something. That was, in fact, quite a lot.
